content.full_text:
BRUNICK, ALVAREZ & BATTERSBY
PROFESSIONAL LAW CORPORATION

WILLIAM J. BRUNICK
DONALD R. ALVAREZ
MARGUERITE P. BATTERSBY
JAMES W. ANDERSON

1839 COMMERCENTER WEST
POST OFFICE BOX 6425
SAN BERNARDINO, CALIFORNIA 92412
TELEPHONE: AREA CODE 714

889-8301 824-0623

KAREN B. MCHUGH
STEVEN M. KENNEDY
FREDERICK L. GERBERT
AMY GREYSON
BOYD L. HILL
LELAND P. MCELHANEY
ELIZABETH O. IRWIN

215 CAJON STREET
P.O. BOX 1320
REDLANDS, CALIFORNIA 92373
TELEPHONE (714) 793-0818

PLEASE REFER TO

7MW-0015

August 4, 1992

FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION
428 J Street, Suite 800
P.O. Box 807
Sacramento, CA 95804-0807

Re: Request for Advice pursuant to Government Code Section
83114(b)

To the Commission:

On behalf of Mojave Water Agency Board of Director members John
Russell and George Parker, I am requesting advice regarding each of
their duties and responsibilities under the conflict-of-interest
provisions of the Political Reform Act. The facts which we believe
are pertinent to this request are as follows:

FACTS

John Russell and George Parker are each members of the Board of
Directors of Mojave Water Agency ("MWA"). Director Russell has
served continuously in this capacity since 1982, after serving an
appointed term from approximately 1970 to 1978. Director Parker
was elected to the Board of Directors in November 1991.

MWA is located in the County of San Bernardino, and is a public
agency organized and existing under Chapter 2146 of Statutes of
1959, known as the Mojave Water Agency Law of 1959 of the State of
California (the "Act"). Pursuant to the Act, MWA is authorized to
take such actions as are necessary to ensure that sufficient water
may be available for the present or future beneficial use or uses
of the lands and inhabitants located within MWA. MWA's power
includes the right to appropriate water, and to acquire and protect
rights for any beneficial purpose, and the right to store,
regulate, control, transport, divert and distribute water for use
within MWA.

In this regard, MWA is statutorily authorized to commence,
maintain, defend in and compromise any action in the State courts,
involving the use, ownership or supply of water, water rights, or

Fair Political Practices Commission
August 4, 1992
Page -2-


water service within MWA as well as outside of MWA, where such
water, water rights, or water service is or may be used, or is
useful, for any purpose within MWA, or involves or affects the
interference or diminution of water flow used or useful for any
purpose within MWA. By statute, MWA is authorized to adjudicate
all rights to extract water affecting water supply within its
boundaries and to determine the natural safe yield of ground water
Basins and stream systems affecting the water supply within the
MWA.

In May 1990, a complaint for declaratory and injunctive relief, and
a petition for writ of mandamus, was filed in the San Bernardino
County Superior Court entitled City of Barstow and the Southern
California Water Company vs. City of Adelanto, et al. (Case No.
BCV6672). There were approximately 150 named defendants, including
MWA. By their action, the plaintiffs, who use water from the
Mojave River System, sought to have the court determine the amounts
of water from the Mojave River System that the City of Barstow and
Southern California Water Company are entitled to have flowing to
them through the Barstow Gauging Station. The plaintiffs alleged
that the Mojave River System is in a state of overdraft due to
cumulative water production by each defendant and upstream of
Barstow, and that the defendants' actions are thereby impairing the
water supply to the plaintiffs.¹

In June, 1991, MWA filed a cross-complaint entitled Mojave Water
Agency v. Anderson, et al., in the above action. A first amended
cross-complaint was then filed in July 1991. In addition to all
parties included in the Barstow complaint, MWA's cross-complaint
named all parties which would be significantly affected by the
relief sought in the Barstow complaint, including parties
downstream of Barstow, who were not named as plaintiffs or
defendants in the complaint (including individuals, cities, county
and/or county entities, water districts, and private parties). The
named cross-defendants include those who pump at least 5-acre feet
each year.² The cross-defendants include Board of Director Parker.
At the time the cross-complaint was filed, Director Parker had not
yet been elected to the Board of Directors of MWA.

¹Overdraft is a condition whereby more water is being
taken from the ground than is being replenished, and if
continued over a period of time, can result in permanent
damage to the groundwater resource.

²An acre-foot is equal to 325,900 gallons, or the amount
of water used by a family of five in one year.

Fair Political Practices Commission
August 4, 1992
Page -3-


The purpose of MWA's cross-complaint was and is to obtain an
adjudication of water rights within MWA and the Mojave River area
of influence. The adjudication is necessary to ensure that the
water presently being taken from the system exceeds the maximum
quantity of water which can be produced annually from the available
supply without unreasonably depleting and causing the eventual
destruction of the ground water as a source of water supply for the
area.

In September 1991, MWA filed a motion to stay the entire action for
one year, or until November 1, 1992. That stay allows a standstill
on pleadings and prevents discovery (except limited discovery by
MWA) and summary judgment motions. The stay allows one year for
the parties collectively to complete engineering studies of the
water supply within the Mojave River and its groundwater basins.
The stay was ordered by the court on October 15, 1991.³

The effect of the MWA's cross-complaint will be to adjudicate all
water rights to the Mojave River System, including the rights of
any and all persons who own property in this area, and who now, or
in the future, will extract water for their personal or business
use. The adjudication would give an adjudicated water right to
each extractor in the action; and each extractor would have to pay
for replacement water to recharge what they produce in excess of
the safe yield. The water right of a particular cross-defendant
may be diminished to a percentage of what they claim at this time;
the actual water rights will not be known or determined until the
engineering studies have been completed.

The effect of a vote for adjudication is to vote for payment on a
net basis for any water extracted beyond the individual user's base
percentage right of the total safe yield. The adjudication will
not financially benefit any pumper.

John Russell is not a defendant or cross-defendant in the pending
action. In addition to being an elected member of the Board of
Directors of MWA, he is also a member of the Board of Directors of
two mutual non-profit water companies. John Russell serves as
a member of the Board of Directors of Center Water Company, located
in Lucerne Valley. He also is on the Board of Directors of Jubilee
Mutual Water Company, located about 4 miles east of Lucerne Valley.
He and his wife also own a share in the Bar-M Mutual Water Company,
located five miles east of Lucerne Valley. Two of these water

_______________________
³In November 1990, the entire case was transferred to
Riverside County Superior Court, where it is presently.

Fair Political Practices Commission
August 4, 1992
Page -4-


companies have been named as Roes Defendants to the MWA's cross-
complaint.

Director and Mrs. Russell's ownership of the share in Bar-H Mutual
Water Company is based upon their ownership of a residential lot
within the water company's area.  This water company covers two
streets, one mile long each.  There are 48 parcels in the water
company, and each parcel has one vote.  He and his wife have owned
the parcel for approximately one and one half years.

Center Water Company is a small water companies that pumps
approximately 10-acre feet per year.  Director Russell has five
shares in Center Water Company.  He has owned a duplex property
(worth around $100,000) and a vacant lot (worth approximately
$45,000) in the Center Water Company jurisdiction for twenty years.
He has also been an owner of the Lucerne Valley Shopping Center for
35 years.  A general partnership (of which he is a general
partner), owns approximately one acre of the shopping center,
containing the parking lot and some buildings.  A family trust owns
the other portion of the shopping center; he owns 1/3, and a
relative, Elma Ragensberg owns 2/3 in a trust.  The partnership
leases the buildings to the shopping center.  The book value of his
interest in the shopping center is more than $1,000, but less than
$100,000.  The fair market value of the shopping center property is
approximately $400,000 (based on a bank appraisal done last year).

Jubilee Water Company pumps just above the 10-acre foot minimum
each year.  Each 1-1/4 parcel has one share in Jubilee Water
Company.  There are a total of 516 shares in Jubilee Water Company,
with 512 active shares.  Director Russell owns one share in Jubilee
Water Company, which he has owned for twenty years.  The shares
have approximately $100 par value, although Director Russell
believes they do not have negotiable value because they are
appurtenant to the property.

Director Russell and his wife also live on a 40-acre parcel in
Lucerne Valley.  They pump under 10-acre feet of water each year.

Director Russell holds the Bar-H property, the house and the
shopping center in joint tenancy with his wife.  He owns the other
properties outright.

It is anticipated that the adjudication of water rights in the
cross-complaint will have little effect on the water rates paid by
the Shopping Center, because the use is a commercial use.  However,
it is anticipated that the adjudication may increase residential
water rates, although the amount of increase cannot be determined

Fair Political Practices Commission
August 4, 1992
Page -5-


at this time.

George Parker is a cross-defendant in the pending lawsuit.
Director Parker owns and lives on a 180-acre farm in the MWA area.
He farms between 20-25 acres at any one time. He grows horse hay,
and has one customer. His gross income has gone as high as $8,000
to $9,000 per year, but is currently approximately $3,000 to
$4,000. The income from the hay business is less than 10% of his
and his wife's total annual income, and does not provide him with
any profit.

During one year, Director Parker's maximum pumping amount is
approximately 174 acre-feet. Last year he pumped 104 acre-feet.
There are three wells on his property. As a result of the
adjudication, he will cut back on how many acres he farms, and
thus, how much water he uses.

Please let me know if this office can provide any additional
information, or be of further assistance.

Very truly yours,

BRUNICK, ALVAREZ & BATTERSBY

Amy Greyson

AG:ml

cc: John Russell, Director, MWA
George Parker, Director, MWA
Larry Rowe, General Manager, MWA

sections.question_synthetic: What are the conflict-of-interest duties and responsibilities of Mojave Water Agency Board members John Russell and George Parker under the Political Reform Act, given their various property interests and the pending water rights adjudication lawsuit that may affect their personal and business interests?
sections.parsing_notes: Format: modern; LLM: This is an incoming request letter from counsel to FPPC, not an FPPC response. No QUESTION or CONCLUSION sections are formally marked, but the request structure is clear. Document contains detailed factual recitation supporting the advice request. No FPPC response/analysis present in this document.

embedding.qa_text: QUESTION: What are the conflict-of-interest duties and responsibilities of Mojave Water Agency Board members John Russell and George Parker under the Political Reform Act, given their various property interests and the pending water rights adjudication lawsuit that may affect their personal and business interests?

embedding.summary: A law firm requests FPPC advice on behalf of two Mojave Water Agency Board members regarding their conflict-of-interest obligations under the Political Reform Act. The request details extensive facts about a pending water rights adjudication lawsuit and the directors' property holdings and financial interests that may be affected by MWA Board decisions.
